coredata.dc:description: The strong excitonic effect significantly influences the efficiency of electron-hole pair generation. Therefore, promoting the dissociation of excitons into free charge carriers has drawn much attention. In this study, nitrogen (N)-doped BiOBr photocatalyst (BOBNC) modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized via a solvothermal method. We demonstrated through photoluminescence and photoelectrochemical techniques the synergistic effect of oxygen vacancies and CQDs, facilitating exciton dissociation and charge carrier migration, consequently significantly enhancing the electron density in the material. Compared to pure BiOBr, degradation experiments revealed that the optimized doping ratio of 0.5BOBNC with CQDs increased the rate constant for sulfamethoxazole (SIZ) by 19.89 times. Furthermore, based on quenching experiments, electron spin resonance (ESR) tests, and DFT calculations, h+, O2 •− and 1O2 were identified as the primary reactive species for SIZ degradation, and a photocatalytic mechanism was proposed. Additionally, the influence of various environmental factors on the photocatalytic system was investigated. In conclusion, this work not only contributes to a profound understanding of BiOBr exciton dissociation but also presents a promising photocatalytic technique for the remediation of diverse water environments.

head.ce:title: Insights into nitrogen-doped BiOBr with oxygen vacancy and carbon quantum dots photocatalysts for the degradation of sulfonamide antibiotics: Actions to promote exciton dissociation and carrier migration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The strong excitonic effect significantly influences the efficiency of electron-hole pair generation. Therefore, promoting the dissociation of excitons into free charge carriers has drawn much attention. In this study, nitrogen (N)-doped BiOBr photocatalyst (BOBNC) modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized via a solvothermal method. We demonstrated through photoluminescence and photoelectrochemical techniques the synergistic effect of oxygen vacancies and CQDs, facilitating exciton dissociation and charge carrier migration, consequently significantly enhancing the electron density in the material. Compared to pure BiOBr, degradation experiments revealed that the optimized doping ratio of 0.5BOBNC with CQDs increased the rate constant for sulfamethoxazole (SIZ) by 19.89 times. Furthermore, based on quenching experiments, electron spin resonance (ESR) tests, and DFT calculations, h+, O2 •− and 1O2 were identified as the primary reactive species for SIZ degradation, and a photocatalytic mechanism was proposed. Additionally, the influence of various environmental factors on the photocatalytic system was investigated. In conclusion, this work not only contributes to a profound understanding of BiOBr exciton dissociation but also presents a promising photocatalytic technique for the remediation of diverse water environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the issue of antibiotic pollution in aquatic environments has become increasingly severe, drawing widespread attention. Involving antibiotic resistance and the threat posed to health by consuming food with residual antibiotics [1,2]. Sulfisoxazole (SIZ) is a broad-spectrum sulfonamide antibiotic (SA) widely used in clinical medicine and animal husbandry. However, due to the inefficiency of traditional wastewater treatment processes, incompletely treated antibiotics are discharged into water bodies, resulting in the accumulation of large amounts of antibiotic residues in various water bodies [3]. Reports have shown that the maximum concentration of SIZ detected in sewage treatment plants in China reaches up to 157 ng/L [4]. High concentrations of SIZ exposed in the environment can pose harm to organisms, plants, etc., leading to the emergence of new antibiotic-resistant genes and accumulation in the food chain. This ultimately poses a threat to human health and the entire ecosystem [5]. Therefore, exploring an efficient and environmentally friendly method to degrade SIZ in various water environments is crucial.

In recent decades, various methods have been employed to mitigate antibiotic pollution in the environment, encompassing adsorption, photocatalysis, thermal catalysis, electrocatalysis, piezoelectric catalysis, and biodegradation [6–9]. Among these, semiconductor photocatalytic technology has demonstrated significant potential in addressing environmental pollution by converting solar energy into chemical energy [10]. For photocatalytic performance, it is widely believed that it is closely related to the separation of photogenerated charge carriers [11–13]. However, this widely recognized knowledge overlooks the excitonic effects during the photodexcitation process, especially for some low-dimensional materials. Excitons are bound electron-hole pairs formed by the interaction of their own Coulomb forces. During the photodexcitation process, they are entangled in energy and cannot be spatially separated, severely impeding the generation of free charges [14–16]. Considering the competition between excitons and carriers during photoexcitation, a rational material design can effectively dissociate excitons into free electrons and holes, thereby achieving considerable quantum yields of carriers [17,18]. In this case, exploring optimized strategies for exciton dissociation become meaningful to enhance charge-carrier-involved photocatalytic performance.

Among various semiconductors, BiOBr has garnered significant attention as an excellent new photocatalyst owing to its distinctive layered structure, appropriate energy band, sound structural integrity, robust photochemical stability, and exceptional oxidizing ability [19,20]. However, the strong exciton effect in BiOBr, resulting in low carrier separation efficiency, coupled with its inherent limitation in the visible light reactive region, imposes constraints on the photocatalytic activity [21,22].

Recent studies have shown that inducing energy-disordered domains in BiOBr proves to be an effective strategy, reducing exciton stability and expediting the dissociation of excitons into free charges. These strategies encompass the construction of heterojunctions, defect engineering, elemental doping, and surface boron percolation [1,23,24]. Recently, some studies have found that doping non-metallic nitrogen (N) elements is also an effective approach to overcome the above inherent limitations [25]. Firstly, the weak van der Waals forces between BiOBr layers facilitate the insertion of N elements into the interlayer space [26], where they hybridize with surrounding metal and oxygen orbitals, forming new energy levels in the valence band, thus reducing the band gap and promoting stronger light absorption [27,28]. Additionally, N-doped induces the formation of defects in the material structure [27,29], which can disrupt the balance of electronic structure, creating energy-disordered domains and inducing exciton dissociation [14].

Although N-doped BiOBr effectively enhances photocatalytic performance, it still lacks excellent charge transfer capability. Fortunately, the combination of semiconductor and carbon materials offers a viable solution to address this issue [30]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been extensively utilized in photocatalysis as co-catalysts with excellent electrochemical properties, enabling them to serve as electron acceptors and facilitate electron transfer [31]. Notably, previous studies have reported that CQDs and oxygen vacancies act synergistically, leading to enhanced charge separation efficiency and transferability in photocatalysts [32–34]. Inspired by these findings, we propose a straightforward strategy to induce abundant oxygen vacancies on the surface of BiOBr through nitrogen doping and to introduce CQDs with excellent electron transfer capabilities. This combination can yield synergistic effects, enhancing exciton dissociation and facilitating carrier migration.

Here, we conducted the following studies to address the aforementioned limitations. CQDs-modified N-doped BiOBr photocatalysts (BOBNC) were prepared through a straightforward hydrothermal method. (1) The composites were characterized through analytical techniques, including SEM, TEM/EDS, HRTEM, XRD, XPS and Raman. (2) The exciton dissociation and carrier migration behavior of 0.5BOBNC were investigated using fluorescence and phosphorescence techniques, along with photoelectrochemical measurements. (3) The photoactivity of the catalyst for the blue light degradation of SIZ was investigated. (4) Quenching and activity detection experiments were conducted and integrated with DFT to elucidate the degradation mechanism. (5) The catalyst's mineralization ability was examined using 3D fluorescence and liquid mass analysis techniques to identify intermediates and determine the degradation pathways. (6) The investigation explored the impact of varying concentrations of SIZ and catalyst, as well as different water and water quality parameters (pH, anions, cations, dissolved organic matter), and diverse pollutants on the degradation of 0.5BOBNC. In summary, the photocatalytic system based on 0.5BOBNC is expected to develop into a promising photoremediation technology.
Refer to Text S1 for an exhaustive list of pertinent experimental agents.

CQDs were synthesized following our previous report; for details, refer to Text S2 [35]. In this paper, N-doped BiOBr photocatalyst materials modified with CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. The synthesis procedure was as follows: Liquid A involved dissolving 1 mmol of Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O and 2 mmol of mannitol in 20 mL of ethylene glycol. For liquid B, 1 mmol of [C16mim]Br and 0.2 g of urea were dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol. Liquid A was then mixed with liquid B and stirred for 10 min. A solution containing CQDs (2 g/L) in varying volumes of 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1 mL was added and continuously stirred for 2 h to produce xBOBNC (x = 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1), with CQDs doped at concentrations of 0.16 wt%, 0.33 wt%, 0.5 wt%, and 0.65 wt%, respectively. The mixture underwent a reaction in a PTFE-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 24 h. Finally, the synthesized material was collected, washed three times with Di water and ethanol, and dried for 12 h. N-doped BiOBr, denoted as BOBN, was prepared using the same method in the absence of CQDs. Pure BiOBr was free of CQDs and urea.
Detailed characterization techniques can be found in the Text S3.

The photodegradation of sulfisoxazole (SIZ) by BiOBr, BOBN, and xBOBNC (0.2 g/L) was investigated under a 9 W blue LED lamp (6.5 mW cm−2; emission wavelength 450–455 nm). The concentration of SIZ was 10 mg/L, and that of the catalyst was 0.2 g/L. The liquids were stirred in darkness for an additional 30 min to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium before exposure to blue light. Subsequently, 1.0 mL of the upper liquid layer was sampled through a 0.45 μm filter tip at specified intervals, and the SIZ concentration was measured by high-performance liquid chromatography (LC-20A, Shimadzu, Japan). The degradation by-products of SIZ were then identified by HRAM LC-MS/MS (refer to Text S5 and Text S6 for specific data).

Theoretical calculations in this study were conducted within the density functional theory framework utilizing the projector-augmented wave method. The exchange–correlation potential was determined using the generalized gradient approximation proposed by Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE). Detailed data can be found in Text S7.

The samples were fractionated to observe surface appearance and internal structure utilizing SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a-b, Both BiOBr and BOBN exhibited similar flower-like microsphere structures, with no apparent morphological differences between them. However, the surface of 0.5BOBNC (Fig. 1c) became less smooth after the addition of CQDs, possibly due to the impregnation of carbon dots [36]. Furthermore, these flower-like microspheres were composed of ultrathin, two-dimensional nanosheet-like structures extending radially outward from the center of the petals. The formation of ultrathin nanosheets was attributed to the dissolution of Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O in mannitol, forming a stable Bi-mannitol complex. Additionally, the reaction with Br ions in [C16mim]Br resulted in the formation of BiOBr cores, which impeded the nanosheet formation and reduced the particle size [37].

The microstructure of 0.5BOBNC was further examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1d depicted a TEM image of a 0.5BOBNC, illustrating its microfloral structure. Fig. 1f displayed the HRTEM image, revealing the crystal structure of 0.5BOBNC with a lattice spacing of 0.277 nm, well-matched with the (110) crystal plane [38]. Additionally, numerous new crystals with a lattice spacing of 0.321 nm were identified, corresponding to (002) crystal planes associated with CQDs [39]. Subsequently, the elemental composition and distribution of the 0.5BOBNC sample were examined through EDX elemental mapping. The elemental mapping (Fig. S1b–f) detected Bi, O, Br, C, and N in the photocatalyst, confirming the successful complexation of N and CQDs onto BiOBr.

As depicted in Fig. S2, the crystal structures of the samples were analyzed via XRD. The XRD patterns of BOBN and 0.5BOBNC aligned with the structure of BiOBr, signifying the absence of other phases. This suggested that the fundamental structure of BiOBr remained unaltered with N-doped and the modification of CQDs. The characteristic peaks at 10.8◦, 25.3◦, 31.7◦, 32.2◦, 39.4◦, 46.2◦, and 57.1◦ can be accurately attributed to the (001), (101), (102), (110), (112), (200), and (212) planes of BiOBr [40]. However, the limited presence of CQDs and N elements hindered the observation of corresponding characteristic diffraction peaks.

The XPS results further validated the coexistence of N elemental and CQDs. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Bi 4f (Fig. 2 a), BiOBr exhibited two typical peaks at 159.1 eV and 164.4 eV, whereas BOBN and 0.5BOBNC were shifted toward lower binding energies. Similarly, in Fig. 2b, the Br peak of BiOBr was resolved into two peaks at 68.3 eV and 69.3 eV, while BOBN and 0.5BOBNC also showed a slight shift toward lower binding energies [41]. Fig. 2c illustrated that the high-resolution spectra of C 1s were resolved into three peaks at 288.4, 284.8, and 286.0 eV. The peak at 284.8 eV was attributed to the orbital C-C or C = C, while the XPS peaks of C 1s centered at 286.0 eV and 288.4 eV binding energies were attributed to C-O and C = O, respectively. This suggests the deposition of CQDs on the 0.5BOBNC surface [39,42]. As depicted in Fig. 2d, the XPS spectrum of N 1s in BOBN and 0.5BOBNC exhibited a single characteristic peak at 399.7 eV, corresponding to the B-N bond. This characteristic peak confirmed the presence of interstitial nitrogen and successful N-doped [1,43]. In this study, no signal corresponding to N was detected in the BiOBr spectra (Fig. S3), indicating that the N element was introduced in situ through the addition of urea.

FT-IR spectra were utilized to verify the existence of N elemental and CQDs in the hybridized material (Fig. 2e). The characteristic peak at 528 cm−1, connected with the Bi-O elongation mode in BiOBr, shifted to a higher wavenumber after N-doped, indicating the formation of a new Bi-N bond [1]. Meanwhile, the –COO- peak at 1470 cm−1 signified the presence of CQDs [42,44]. Additionally, the absorption peaks at 3432 cm−1 and 2930 cm−1 were attributed to the elongation motions of –OH and C-H, respectively [45,46]. These results confirmed the successful coupling of N elemental and CQDs with BiOBr, aligning with the analytical results of XPS.

We further analyzed the synthesized samples using Raman spectroscopy. As shown in the Fig. S4a, the Raman spectra of the synthesized photocatalysts were recorded in the range of 50 to 1800 cm−1. The Raman bands of BiOBr (Fig. S4b) were mainly located at 56, 107, and 160 cm−1, attributed to the external A1g, internal A1g, and internal E1g stretching modes of Bi-Br bonds in BiOBr material [47]. The band at 84 cm−1 corresponded to the first-order vibration A1g mode of metallic Bi [48]. After nitrogen doping, the overall peak intensities of BOBN and 0.5BOBNC were reduced compared to BiOBr, which was attributed to the increase in the number of oxygen vacancies [49]. Meanwhile, the peaks initially observed at 56, 84, and 160 cm−1 in both samples shifted to 54, 81, and 158 cm−1, respectively, possibly due to the formation of Bi-N bonds altering the chemical milieu of BiOBr [28,50]. It was noteworthy that in Fig. S4c, two new peaks were observed at 1338 and 1590 cm−1 for the 0.5BOBNC sample, corresponding to the D and G bands of CQDs, respectively. The peak at the D band corresponded to the vibrational mode of terminal graphite carbon (sp3) planes, while the G band was attributed to vibrations of carbon atoms with sp2 bonds [51].

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (Fig. 2f) revealed that the adsorption–desorption isotherms of all three catalysts exhibited type IV behavior, indicative of typical porous characteristics [52]. The specific surface areas of BiOBr, BOBN, and 0.5BOBNC were 42.51, 43.93 and 49.64 m2 g−1, respectively. As reported, the superior specific surface area of 0.5BOBNC was attributed to the presence of CQDs [36]. The increased specific surface area provided the material with additional reaction centers and facilitated the transfer of reactants. In summary, the above characterization indicated the successful preparation of 0.5BOBNC through doping with N elemental and CQDs, underscoring the excellent photocatalytic potential of the newly prepared material.

The optical absorption properties of BiOBr, BOBN and 0.5BOBNC were analyzed using UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy to elucidate the impact of N-doped (Fig. 3 a). BOBN (451 nm), compared to pure BiOBr (446 nm), demonstrated a broader optical absorption range and enhanced optical absorption in the visible and near-infrared regions. This enhancement could be attributed to the reduced bandgap resulting from the introduced N-doped [53]. The light absorption capability of 0.5BOBNC (460 nm) was further enhanced by the addition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), possibly due to the upconversion ability of CQDs [35]. In conclusion, UV–vis tests indicated the anticipated excellent activity of the modified material under light.

Initially, we calculated the bandgap of the samples by constructing a Tauc plot (Eq. (1)), wherein BiOBr is an indirect bandgap semiconductor (n = 4) [6]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the bandgap energies (Eg) of BiOBr, BOBN, and 0.5BOBNC were 2.55 eV, 2.39 eV, and 2.29 eV, respectively.

Subsequently, the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) positions of BiOBr, BOBN, and 0.5BOBNC were determined by Mott-Schottky plots and VB-XPS. The positive slopes of the Mott-Schottky curves in Fig. 3c-e indicated that BiOBr, BOBN, and 0.5BOBNC were all n-type semiconductors. The flat band potentials of BiOBr, BOBN, and 0.5BOBNC with respect to the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were −0.37 eV, −0.66 eV, and −0.69 eV respectively, and with respect to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) were 0.18 eV, −0.11 eV, and −0.14 eV [23]. Since the flat band potential of n-type materials is approximately equal to the Fermi level, the Fermi levels of BiOBr, BOBN, and 0.5BOBNC with respect to NHE were 0.18 eV, −0.11 eV, and −0.14 eV respectively [54]. Based on the VB-XPS results, the bandgaps between the Fermi level and VB positions of BiOBr, BOBN, and 0.5BOBNC were calculated to be 2.03 eV, 1.83 eV, and 1.78 eV respectively. Thus, the VB positions of BiOBr, BOBN, and 0.5BOBNC with respect to NHE were calculated to be 2.21 eV, 1.72 eV, and 1.64 eV respectively [6]. Additionally, according to Eq. (2), the CB positions of BiOBr, BOBN, and 0.5BOBNC with respect to NHE were calculated to be −0.31 eV, −0.67 eV, and −0.65 eV respectively. In summary, we had calculated the bandgaps of BiOBr, BOBN, and 0.5BOBNC, as shown in Fig. 3f.

In the XPS spectra of O 1s (Fig. 4 a), the three peaks attributed to BiOBr were detected at 529.9, 531.8, and 533.6 eV, typically associated with lattice oxygen, defect adsorbed oxygen and surface hydroxyl groups [26]. Specifically, BOBN and 0.5BOBNC showed a significantly larger proportion of defect adsorbed oxygen compared to pure BiOBr, indicating the presence of abundant oxygen vacancies. Moreover, the O-orbital binding energies of BOBN and 0.5BOBNC were lower than those of BiOBr, implying enhanced electron density near the oxygen vacancies [16]. Furthermore, this alteration disrupted the equilibrium of the surrounding electronic structure, augmented the degree of disorder, and provided the impetus for exciton separation [17].

we utilized EPR spectroscopy to further confirm the presence of oxygen vacancies [55]. EPR signals are generated by electrons trapped at oxygen vacancies, with stronger signals indicating higher concentrations of oxygen vacancies, as the presence of oxygen vacancies is accompanied by the appearance of unpaired electrons. As depicted in Fig. 4b, all three samples showed EPR signals at g = 2.002, indicating the presence of oxygen vacancies [1]. Notably, BOBN and 0.5BOBNC exhibited stronger intensity, suggesting that N-doped simultaneously promoted the generation of more oxygen vacancies in the material. This not only facilitated the activation of O2 but also effectively induced exciton dissociation, promoting carrier production [56].

Steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy (PL) is widely acknowledged as an effective method for studying exciton dissociation. As the fluorescence intensity arises from the attenuation of singlet excitons, it enables the visualization of exciton decay [13]. Exciton processes in the samples were investigated using PL spectroscopy. Fig. 4c illustrated a substantial decrease in PL intensity for both BOBN and 0.5BOBNC compared to BiOBr. This decrease was attributed to the abundant oxygen vacancy defects inducing a large number of exciton dissociations [21]. In contrast, 0.5BOBNC exhibited the lowest PL intensity after the addition of CQDs, demonstrating that CQDs could further enhance exciton dissociation by improving electron transfer ability [57].

Typically, singlet excitons undergo intersystem crossover transitions to triplet excitons or dissociate into free charges [58,59]. Thus, steady-state phosphorescence (PH) spectroscopy could offer more detailed insights as the attenuation of triplet excitons emit phosphorescence information [60]. As depicted in Fig. 4d, the emergence of peaks substantiated the presence of triple excitons in BiOBr, BOBN, and 0.5BOBNC. The lowest PH signal in 0.5BOBNC indicated the least number of triple excitons. This suggested that the main reason of the decrease in singlet excitons in 0.5 BOBNC was the dissociation of these excitons into free charges.

The Mott-Schottky formula (Eq. (3)) can be used to estimate the carrier concentration [17]. Through calculation, the carrier concentration of 0.5BOBNC was 2.79 times higher than the BiOBr. The prominent increase in carrier concentration constitutes strong evidence of enhanced exciton dissociation.

Here, B is the prefactor and τ is the radiation lifetime.

The average lifetimes calculated from the PH kinetics were 3.52 ns, 2.32 ns, and 1.96 ns, respectively (Fig. 4e). The shorter lifetime of BOBN singlet excitons compared to BiOBr suggested that abundant oxygen vacancies improved exciton dissociation [60,61]. With the addition of CQDs, the singlet excitons lifetime of 0.5BOBNC was further shortened. This could be attributed to the formation of electron channels between BOBN and CQDs, facilitating the rapid transfer of electrons to the CQDs and effectively promoting the secession of electron-hole, thereby facilitating the dissociation of excitons.

Carrier separation and transport were investigated through the analysis of transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As observed in Fig. 4f, 0.5BOBNC exhibited the highest photocurrent density, further confirming efficient dissociation of excitons into charge carriers and an increase in electron concentration [16]. Additionally, 0.5BOBNC showed the minimum radius in the EIS graphic, indicating the lowest carrier transmission resistance, consequently, the highest carrier transfer productivity. This confirmed that the modification of CQDs significantly decreased the interfacial hindrance and enhanced carrier transportation in 0.5BOBNC [10]. Based on these results, we demonstrated that exciton dissociation and carrier transfer in 0.5BOBNC could be effectively enhanced through N-doped and CQDs-modified.

To elucidate the electron transfer paths in 0.5BOBNC, DFT calculations were conducted. BOBN primarily exhibited a tendency to lose electrons at the interface, with electron enrichment observed near the CQDs (Fig. 5 a-b). In Fig. 5c, the difference in plane-averaged charge densities in the z-axis was presented, with positive values indicating electron accumulation and negative values indicating electron depletion. The variation at the interface clearly indicated electron flow from the BOBN side across the interface into the CQDs side, with holes remaining on the BOBN side. This suggested the migration of photogenerated electrons from BOBN to CQDs [62]. Additionally, the confirmation that CQDs served as electron acceptors in the 0.5BOBNC system enhanced the electron transfer ability, resulting in significantly improved carrier separation and further promotion of exciton dissociation.

In this study, we investigated the photodegradation properties of the prepared materials, with a focus on sulfisoxazole (SIZ), one of the most widely used antibiotics. After dark adsorption for 30 min, all samples reached SIZ adsorption-resolution equilibrium, as illustrated in Fig. S5. Notably, 0.5BOBNC exhibited an adsorption of 14.2 % for SIZ, surpassing BOBN (6.6 %) and BiOBr (1.8 %). This superiority arises from the larger specific surface area of 0.5BOBNC, facilitating increased SIZ adsorption. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, the concentration of SIZ maintained virtually constant during a 15-minute exposure to light, confirming its optical stability. In contrast, BiOBr presented feeble catalytic activity, yielding a degradation rate of 25.6 % in 15 min. Conversely, BOBN demonstrated a stronger catalytic activity of 67.8 %. Notably, 0.5BOBNC exhibited the most robust photocatalytic activity, achieving complete SIZ degradation within 15 min. The degradation efficiency escalated from 67.8 % to 99.7 %, with a photocatalytic rate (kobs) reaching 0.3838 min−1. However, the photocatalytic degradation ability exhibited an initial increase and subsequent decrease with varying CQDs amounts. While increased CQDs enhanced electron conduction, an excessive amount could obscure active sites on the 0.5BOBNC nanosheets, impeding light absorption and inhibiting the degradation effect. Fig. 6b illustrated the rate constants for the degradation of SIZ by various materials were presented. Notably, 0.5BOBNC exhibited the highest rate constant (0.3838 min−1), surpassing those of BOBN (0.0763 min−1) and BiOBr (0.0193 min−1) by 5.03 and 19.89 times, respectively. Additionally, the 0.5BOBNC photocatalytic system demonstrated superior SIZ degradation compared to other photocatalytic systems (Table. S3).

Photocatalytic stability is a crucial metric for assessing catalyst performance. As depicted in Fig. S6a, after five cycles of the experiment, 0.5BOBNC exhibited a degradation efficiency surpassing 80 % while maintaining robust catalytic activity. This observation provided evidence of the demonstrated reusability of the photocatalyst. Additionally, as indicated by the XRD image (Fig. S6b), the crystal and structure of 0.5BOBNC maintained unvarying after five cycles, affirming its exceptional stability and reusability.

To elucidate the influence of various reactive species (RSs) formed during the degradation of 0.5 BOBNC on the degradation of SIZ, we conducted quenching experiments using four quenchers. The quenchers for h+, 1O2, O2 •−, and •OH were Na2C2O4, L-Histidine, BQ, and TBA, respectively. The impacts of various quenchers on the degradation of SIZ by 0.5BOBNC varied. As depicted in Fig. 6c, the degradation constants of SIZ were 0.0329, 0.0424, 0.0161, and 0.2195 min−1 for the solutions containing Na2C2O4, L-Histidine, BQ, and TBA, respectively, after 15 min of light exposure. The contributions of h+, 1O2, O2 •−, and •OH to the degradation of SIZ were 91.45 %, 88.95 %, 95.75 %, and 42.36 %, respectively. (The total contribution exceeded 100 %, possibly owing to the interconversion between active species during photocatalysis [62]). These findings indicated that h+, 1O2, and O2 •− played crucial roles in the degradation of SIZ within the 0.5BOBNC photocatalytic, with •OH also contributing to the process.

The production of RSs through photocatalysis can be verified using ESR testing. As shown in Fig. 6d-f, all three materials exhibited ESR signals of varying intensities under light conditions, indicating the formation of O2 •−,1O2, and •OH during the photocatalytic process. Additionally, all the ESR signals of 0.5BOBNC were stronger than those of BiOBr and BOBN, suggesting that the modification with CQDs and N-doped increased the generation of extra RSs. The signal intensities of these three reactive species increased with prolonged exposure time, indicating their successive generation by the 0.5BOBNC photocatalyst during light exposure and highlighting its enhanced ability to activate molecular oxygen.

To assess the activation capacity of molecular oxygen by 0.5BOBNC, NBT conversion was employed to quantify the concentration of superoxide [63]. From Fig. S7a, the UV–visible absorption of NBT gradually decreased over time with increasing illumination time. The generation of superoxide was calculated to be 4.83 × 10-6 M, 9.12 × 10-6 M, and 12.48 × 10-6 M, showing a 2.6 times enhancement in 0.5BOBNC compared to BiOBr. This result demonstrates the excellent superoxide generation capability of 0.5BOBNC composites. Secondly, O2 or N2 continuous bubbling experiments (Fig. S7b) revealed a significant decrease in the degradation rate of 0.5BOBNC under an N2 atmosphere, suggesting that O2 played a crucial role in the transformation of RSs. Finally, the adsorption capacity of oxygen was evaluated for all three samples using the O2-TPD technique (Fig. S8). The desorption peak of 0.5BOBNC shifted to higher temperatures compared to BiOBr and BOBN, suggesting a stronger interaction between 0.5BOBNC and oxygen, thereby lowering the energy barrier for oxygen activation [60]. These results demonstrated the outstanding molecular oxygen activation capability of 0.5BOBNC composites.

According to previous reports, the formation of 1O2 can be divided into two pathways. One pathway involves the activation of molecular oxygen through energy transfer in the exciton effect, while the other pathway involves the formation of 1O2 through the binding of superoxide to holes [14]. To verify the generation of 1O2, we observed that the signal of BiOBr remained unchanged after quenching the superoxide by the addition of benzoquinone (BQ) (Fig. S9a), suggesting a strong exciton effect in BiOBr. In contrast, the 1O2 signal of 0.5BOBNC was significantly reduced (Fig. S9b), indicating that 1O2 formation in the 0.5BOBNC photocatalytic system mainly occurred through the superoxide pathway rather than energy transfer [14,23,64].

Based on the above results, we deduced the degradation mechanism of 0.5BOBNC (Fig. 7 ). Initially, photogenerated carriers were generated on the surface of BOBN under light. The electrons were excited to jump into the CB of BOBN, and the holes stayed in the VB. However, due to the interaction of their own Coulomb force, these electron-hole pairs easily entangled to form excitons. Fortunately, the abundance of oxygen vacancies altered the distribution of electrons around the vacancies, disrupting the energetic ordering. This alteration provided the impetus for exciton dissociation, allowing the exciton to dissociate into a free charge. Additionally, the effective transfer of electrons from the BOBN was facilitated by the excellent charge transfer capability of CQDs, effectively inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated charges, further enhancing exciton dissociation. Considering the CB potential (−0.65 eV) was more negative compared to the reduction potential (−0.33 eV) of E°(O2/O2 •−), electrons could react with O2 to form O2 •− (Eq. (7)). A portion of O2 •− reacted with h+ to form 1O2 (Eq. (8)). However, as OH–/•OH (1.99 eV) was more negative than the VB of 0.5BOBNC (1.64 eV), O2 •− could only react by first generating H2O2 with H+ in water (Eq. (9)) and then decomposing to •OH (Eq. (10)). Ultimately, the SIZ molecule underwent attacks by h+, 1O2, O2 •−, and •OH, leading to degradation and mineralization (Eq. (11)).
0.5BOBNC + hv → e- + h+(6)
O2 + e- → O2 •−(7)
O2 •−+h+ → 1O2(8)
O2 •−+e-+2H+ → H2O2(9)
H2O2 + e- OH–+•OH(10)
(h+, 1O2, O2 •−, •OH) + SIZ → Products(11)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of environmental factors on photocatalytic systems
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 8 a, the adsorption of SIZ under light-free conditions rose from 7.1 % to 34.3 % with the concentration of 0.5BOBNC rose from 0.1 g/L to 0.8 g/L, while the SIZ degraded in blue light escalated from 85.2 % to 100 %. These outcomes were ascribed to the enlarged contact area and increased number of adsorption sites. However, when the amount of injected 0.5BOBNC reached 1.2 g/L, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency did not exhibit a significant increase, despite 45.6 % of SIZ was adsorbed. This lack of improvement may be attributed to the excessive amount of photocatalyst hindering light penetration.

Fig. 8b explored the effect of different SIZ concentrations on degradation. The catalytic activity of 0.5 BOBNC gradually decreased with the increasing in SIZ dosage. With the elevation of the SIZ dosage from 20 mg/L to 60 mg/L, the rate reduced from 0.1148 min−1 to 0.0549 min−1. On the one hand, the high concentration of SIZ may absorb some the incident light as the concentration increases during the reaction, thus reducing the number of photons activating BiOBr. On the other hand, the intermediate products may lead to the depletion of free radicals, which ultimately triggered the decrease of catalytic activity [65].

Additionally, the initial pH in the aqueous solution had a significant influence on the photocatalytic performance. The impact of varying pH on photocatalytic activity was investigated through the addition of HNO3 and NaOH, as illustrated in Fig. 8c. The initial pH of the SIZ solution was 4.73, and the photocatalytic activity showed minimal change upon adjusting the pH to 3. However, with an increase in pH from 4.73 to 11, the reaction rate constant decreased from 0.3838 min−1 to 0.02176 min−1. The surface charge of 0.5BOBNC was analyzed using a zeta potential analyzer, and the zero point site was determined to be 5.3. This suggested that the surface of 0.5BOBNC was positively charged when pH < 5.3, while it became negatively charged when pH > 5.3. According to the literature, the pKa of SIZ were 1.5 and 5.0 [2]. Therefore, when the initial pH was between 1.5 and 5, SIZ was not dissociated and has minimal impact on degradation. When pH > 5, SIZ was anionic in solution, and the surface of the catalyst became negatively charged, exhibiting electrostatic repulsion. With a further increase in pH, the repulsion between the catalyst and SIZ intensified, inhibiting the degradation reaction.

It is widely recognized that substances in natural water bodies could interfere with the degradation of SIZ to varying degrees. Fig. 8d illustrated the effects of various common substances present in natural water bodies on the degradation system of 0.5BOBNC. Among these ions, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, SO4 2-, NO3 –, and Cl- had no significant effect on the photocatalytic activity of 0.5BOBNC. However, the presence of HCO3 – significantly influenced the photocatalytic activity of 0.5BOBNC, leading to a decrease in the observed rate constants from 0.3838 min−1 to 0.2010 min−1, respectively. This occurred because HCO3 – competitively reacted with the reactive substances (•OH), thereby affecting the degradation activity [66]. Additionally, the presence of Dissolved Organic Matter (DOM) exhibited a significant inhibition of the photocatalytic activity. This could be attributed to the fact that DOM not only scavenged •OH and O2 •− in the water column but also obstructed the absorption of light by the catalyst [67].

As depicted in Fig. 8e, the application of the 0.5BOBNC system in various natural water bodies was explored. In comparison to ultrapure water, the other six water qualities exhibited varying degrees of influence on the photocatalytic degradation of SIZ. The existence of organic and inorganic ions in these waters was the reason for this variability. These substances reacted with the active species, ultimately affecting photocatalytic efficiency [60]. Nevertheless, the 0.5BOBNC photocatalytic system achieved removal rates of 95 %, 91.2 %, 71.5 %, 61.8 %, 73 %, and 85 % for SIZ in rainwater, tap water, Pearl River water, seawater, secondary sedimentation tank effluent, and lake water, respectively. This observation suggested that the 0.5BOBNC photocatalytic system maintained robust degradation activity for SIZ in various common natural waters.

The capability of degrading different pollutants in the 0.5BOBNC photocatalytic system was further researched, as depicted in Fig. 8f. The experiments were conducted on NPX, CIP, DCF, and TC under the same conditions. Within 15 min, 0.5BOBNC exhibited excellent degradation performance towards TC and DCF, with removal efficiencies reaching 98 % and 93 %, which were 8.92 times and 8.85 times higher than those of BiOBr, respectively (Fig. S10). The degradation of NPX and CIP was also noteworthy, with removal efficiencies of 88 % and 70 %, respectively, which were 10.58 times and 6.09 times higher than those of BiOBr. In conclusion, the 0.5BOBNC photocatalytic system demonstrated outstanding photocatalytic performance, particularly in treating high concentrations of SIZ, SIZ in different water bodies, and degrading various antibiotics.

We gained insight into the degradation process of SIZ by detecting the residue after the degradation of the 0.5BOBNC system using the 3D EEMs. In Fig. 9 a, the initial SIZ signal was notably weak, attributed to the presence of a sulfonyl group as an electron-withdrawing group in SIZ, significantly affecting the fluorescence intensity of SIZ [68–70]. With the progression of the reaction, the fluorescence intensity significantly increased after 15 min (Fig. 9b), indicating the decomposition of SIZ into a substantial number of intermediates. However, after 90 min of photodegradation (Fig. 9c), the most intense fluorescent centers disappeared, and the fluorescence intensity significantly decreased due to further decomposition or mineralization of intermediates [71]. 3D EEMs tests demonstrated that SIZ could be removed by photodegradation.

The mineralization capacity of the photocatalytic system was assessed by measuring the total organic carbon (TOC). As depicted in Fig. S11, the TOC degradation rate reached 45 % after 1.5 h of blue light irradiation. The findings indicated that the 0.5BOBNC photocatalytic system effectively mineralized SIZ into CO2 and H2O.

The degradation solution's intermediates of SIZ were analyzed using HRAM LC-MS/MS. Table S4 summarizes the detailed information of 11 potential intermediates and their MS/MS results. Four possible SIZ decomposition pathways were deduced (Fig. 9d). The deviation between the mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) and the actual m/z was calculated to be less below 5 ppm, fulfilling the relevant qualitative analysis requirement.

In pathway I, the S-N bond connecting the benzene and azole portions was susceptible to attack by superoxide [67]. Following this, it underwent cleavage, with –OH and −H groups added on both sides, forming P1 (+m/z = 174.0219) and P2 (+m/z = 129.0659), respectively. Subsequent continuous oxidation by the active species led to the formation of P3 (+m/z = 127.0502). This hypothesis is consistent with other studies [72]. In pathway II, the C-N bond of SIZ was susceptible to attack by singlet oxygen, forming compound P4 (+m/z = 173.0379). Afterward, the S-N bond underwent cleavage after attack by superoxide, resulting in the formation of P5 (+m/z = 156.0113), which was ultimately oxidized by the active species to form P6 (+m/z = 110.0600) [5]. In pathway III, the heterocycle was attacked by hydroxyl radicals, undergoing hydroxylation to form P7 (+m/z = 270.2291). Subsequently, under the attack of superoxide, the formation of P1 and P8 (+m/z = 130.0927) occurred [73]. In pathway IV, the attack of active species such as •OH, h+, O2 •−, led to the cleavage of the S-C bond, forming by-products P9 (+m/z = 193.0278) and P6 [54].

In summary, this paper presented the preparation of novel CQDs-modified N-doped BiOBr nanosheets through a simple hydrothermal method. Fluorescence phosphorescence spectroscopy and photoelectrochemical tests revealed that the introduction of N optimized the energy band structure, significantly enhanced light absorption, and introduced abundant oxygen vacancies for exciton dissociation. DFT analysis further demonstrated that the addition of CQDs enhanced electron transfer, strengthening exciton dissociation. According to the Mott-Schottky equation, the electron concentration of 0.5BOBNC was 2.79 times higher than that of BiOBr. This increased yield of exciton dissociation into free carriers contributed to the outstanding photocatalytic activity of 0.5BOBNC (0.3838 min−1) in degrading SIZ, which was 19.89 times higher than that of BiOBr (0.0193 min−1). The main reactive species for sulfisoxazole (SIZ) removal were identified as h+, O2 •−, and 1O2. The study proposed a photocatalytic mechanism and four degradation pathways. Furthermore, the 0.5BOBNC photocatalytic system demonstrated high activity in different natural waters and exhibited excellent degradation effects on NPX, CIP, DCF, and TC. This study not only unveils the exciton effect of photocatalytic materials but also presents a promising photocatalytic technique for the remediation of diverse water environments.
